Let's talk hormones

The 7 Hormones
Running the Whole Show

Seven hormones. We'll go through what each one does, what messes it up, what sorts it out — and the exact habits to ditch and the ones to lock in, so they're working for you instead of against you.

So why listen to me?

Coach Loai & ReShape

[ One line: who you are and who you help. ]

Real ReShape transformations. Not theory — this is the work.
By the time we're done

You'll walk out knowing how to —

  1. 01Name all seven — and actually know what each one's doing.
  2. 02Spot when one's gone off the rails — in you or someone you train.
  3. 03Know what feeds each one and what trashes it — no guessing.
  4. 04Leave with a hit-list: what to bin, what to build.
How we'll run through each one

Same five questions.
Every hormone.

What it does When it's happy When it's struggling What trashes it What helps it The one thing to change

Follow the orange bar at the bottom. See the orange box? That's the bit to write down.

Hormone 1 of 7
Insulin
Your Blood-Sugar Boss

The key that lets sugar into your cells. Keep it sensitive and energy stays steady — let it jam up and you get the crashes, the cravings and the weight round the middle that won't shift.

In one move
Walk after meals. Lift through the week.
the 3pm slump
Insulin
Your Blood-Sugar Boss · Part 1 of 2

What it does: it's the key that unlocks your cells to let sugar in for energy. When the lock works, sugar gets used. When cells stop responding — insulin resistance — the sugar's stuck in your blood.

When it's happy

Steady energy — no 11am or 3pm crash, no hangry shaky meltdown, and you're not thinking about your next snack all day.

When it's struggling

Energy on a rollercoaster: big crashes, hangry and shaky, foggy after meals, constant carb cravings, weight settling round the middle that won't budge.

Insulin
Steady beats spiky · Part 2 of 2

A 10-minute walk after eating literally flattens that red line — muscle pulls the sugar out of your blood.

What helps it
  • Moving — muscle soaks up sugar
  • Fibre and mostly whole food
  • Decent sleep
What trashes it
  • Sitting all day
  • Sugary stuff on repeat
  • Bad sleep + stress
Change this
Walk after you eat. A 10-minute walk after meals + lifting through the week is the biggest lever you've got.
Hormone 2 of 7
Estrogen
The Boss

Runs your cycle — and quietly looks after your bones, heart, brain and skin. The goal is keeping it in range: too low and too high both cause grief.

In one move
Eat enough to keep your cycle.
the hot-flush, flat days
Estrogen
The Boss · Part 1 of 2

What it does: this one's huge. Yeah, it runs your cycle — but it's reaching way further than that. Think of it as the boss touching half your body at once. Way more than just “where you store fat.”

When it's happy

You feel like you — mood's steady, you sleep well, skin's behaving, you actually want sex, and your energy's even right across the month.

When it's struggling

Too little: hot then cold, foggy, flat, broken sleep, sex feels like a chore, periods vanish. Too much vs progesterone: heavy painful periods, sore swollen chest, bloated and irritable.

Estrogen
Keep it in range · Part 2 of 2

Both ends are a problem. The goal isn't “more estrogen” — it's keeping it in range.

What helps it
  • Eating enough + enough body fat
  • Fibre + a healthy gut to clear it
  • Lifting / weight-bearing work
What trashes it
  • Barely eating / body fat too low
  • Too much booze
  • Smoking + being stressed 24/7
Change this
Eat enough. Honestly — under-fuel all the time and your cycle's the first thing to go. Estrogen needs fuel to even show up.
Hormone 3 of 7
Progesterone
The Chill One

Your calm-and-sleep hormone. The catch: you only make it after you ovulate. No ovulation, no progesterone.

In one move
Drop the chronic stress. Stop training on empty.
wide awake at 2am
Progesterone
The Chill One · Part 1 of 2

What it does: here's the catch — it only shows up after you ovulate. It preps the body for pregnancy and acts like your chill pill: better sleep, calmer mood, balances out estrogen.

When it's happy

The week before your period feels manageable — you're calmer, you drift off to sleep easier, mood's level instead of all over the place.

When it's struggling

Low: you lie awake wired, anxious and snapping at everyone before your period, tearful over nothing, spotting. That's the PMS/PMDD picture.

Progesterone
No ovulation, no progesterone · Part 2 of 2

You don't “top up” progesterone with a supplement. You earn it by ovulating — so protect the things that let you.

What helps it
  • Actually ovulating
  • Eating enough + healthy weight
  • Keeping stress down
What trashes it
  • Stress (kills ovulation first)
  • Under-eating / overtraining
  • No ovulation (PCOS, perimenopause)
Change this
Back off the chronic stress and stop smashing yourself in the gym on empty — that's how you actually get progesterone.
Hormone 4 of 7
Testosterone
Drive, Muscle & Bone

Your drive, your muscle, your bones. And the twist for women — too much (think PCOS) is usually the problem, not too little.

In one move
Lift weights. Protect your sleep.
the strength it builds
Testosterone
Drive, Muscle & Bone · Part 1 of 2

What it does: yep, women make it too — just less. It's the engine behind your drive, your muscle and your bones. And the twist: in women, too much is usually the problem, not too little.

When it's happy

You've got drive — you want to train, you want sex, you feel strong, and your sessions actually show up as muscle.

When it's struggling

Too low: zero libido, knackered, can't be bothered, muscle melting away. Too high (usually PCOS): breakouts that won't quit, hair where you don't want it, thinning on top, cycles all over.

Testosterone
It's about balance · Part 2 of 2

In women, the scale usually tips high — and it's almost always riding on insulin. Fix insulin, fix testosterone.

What helps it
  • Lifting weights
  • Sleep + enough protein
  • Keeping insulin in check
What trashes it
  • Running on no sleep
  • Insulin resistance + sitting around (PCOS)
  • Too much booze + stress
Change this
Pick up the weights and protect your sleep — that sorts testosterone whether it's too high or too low.
Hormone 5 of 7
Cortisol
Stress & Wake-Up

Not the villain — you need it to wake up and handle stress. The problem is a broken rhythm: wired at night, wiped in the morning.

In one move
Daylight early. No late coffee. Same bedtime.
wired and stressed
Cortisol
Stress & Wake-Up · Part 1 of 2

What it does: everyone calls it the bad guy — it's not, you need it. It's meant to spike in the morning to wake you up, then wind down by night. The problem isn't cortisol. It's when that curve goes flat.

When it's happy

You wake up actually ready to go, get through a stressful day, then switch off at night and sleep through. Stress hits — then passes.

When it's struggling

Tired-but-wired: knackered all day, then buzzing at midnight. Snappy and anxious, catching every cold, craving sugar by 3pm, softer round the middle.

Cortisol
Fix the rhythm · Part 2 of 2
What helps it
  • Same sleep + wake time, daylight early
  • Breathing, walking, actual people
  • Eating enough
What trashes it
  • Stress on tap
  • Short, broken sleep
  • Overtraining + under-eating; late coffee
Change this
Sort your rhythm: daylight first thing, knock the late coffee on the head, same bedtime every night.
Don't say “adrenal fatigue.” It's not a real diagnosis, and “cortisol detoxes” are just someone selling you something. The actual issue is a broken rhythm — say that.
Hormone 6 of 7
Leptin
The “I’m Full” Signal

Your fat cells telling the brain the tank's full. Being heard matters more than having loads of it.

In one move
Sleep well. Bin the crash diets.
food on the brain
Leptin
The “I'm Full” Signal · Part 1 of 2

What it does: your fat cells make it and ping your brain to say how much fuel is in the tank — the long-game “I'm full” signal. It also runs the cycle: low leptin = “not enough fuel,” and the brain shuts things down.

When it's happy

You feel full after a proper meal and it sticks — appetite's not running the show and food's not constantly on your mind.

When it's struggling

Resistance: hungry even after eating, never quite satisfied, always grazing. Too low (very lean): ravenous, freezing cold, cycle's stopped.

Leptin
Low · just right · ignored · Part 2 of 2

More leptin isn't the goal — being heard is. Crash diets and bad sleep are what break the signal.

What helps it
  • Good sleep
  • Eating steady, no crash diets
  • Keeping active
What trashes it
  • Not sleeping
  • Living on ultra-processed food
  • Extreme dieting
Change this
Bin the crash diets. The all-or-nothing stuff tanks leptin and pretty much guarantees you bounce straight back.
Hormone 7 of 7
Ghrelin
The Hunger Alarm

Your 'feed me' alarm — honest when you're rested, relentless when you're not.

In one move
Protect your 7–9 hours.
the late-night fridge raid
Ghrelin
The Hunger Alarm · Part 1 of 2

What it does: this is your stomach yelling “feed me.” It climbs before meals and drops once you've eaten — a clean rhythm you can trust. Bad sleep and crash diets jam it on so it never shuts up.

When it's happy

Hunger that makes sense — it turns up near mealtimes and switches off once you've eaten. You're not fighting it all day.

When it's struggling

Hungry the second you wake, gnawing all day, food on the brain constantly — the alarm jammed on after bad sleep or a crash diet.

Ghrelin
Sleep runs the dial · Part 2 of 2

Same person, same food — one short night and the alarm screams all day. Sleep is the dial.

What helps it
  • Enough sleep
  • Protein + fibre at meals
  • Eating on a regular rhythm
What trashes it
  • No sleep
  • Crash diets / yo-yo'ing
  • Skipping meals + stress
Change this
Sleep on it. Get your 7–9 hours — one bad night and you'll be starving and fighting cravings all day.
Here's the bit nobody tells you

They don't work on their own.

Chase one on its own and you'll be chasing symptoms forever. They're all wired together.

Stress → blood sugar
Cortisol shoves blood sugar up → insulin goes up. Stay stressed and you slide into insulin resistance.
Insulin ↔ testosterone
High insulin tells the ovaries to pump out more testosterone — that's the engine behind PCOS.
Stress → your cycle
Too much cortisol switches off ovulation, and estrogen and progesterone drop with it.
Fuel → your cycle
Too little food → leptin drops → brain switches your cycle off. Periods stop.
Estrogen → metabolism
Estrogen helps you handle carbs; when it drops at menopause, belly fat creeps in.
Sleep → all of it
One bad night: hunger up, cortisol up, fullness signal down, blood sugar worse. Sleep sits on top of everything.
Forget the “cortisol steal” thing. Stress doesn't “steal” the ingredients for your sex hormones — it messes with your cycle through the brain's signals. That's the real story.
The hit-list

Habits to bin · Habits to build

Bin these

  • Running on no sleep + scrolling till 1am
  • Crash diets and the yo-yo thing
  • Sitting all day, flopping down after every meal
  • Training hard on barely any food
  • Late, heavy coffee and booze most nights
  • Trying to just “push through” stress

Build these

  • Same bed + wake time, daylight first thing
  • Eat enough — protein and fibre on the plate
  • Lift weights 2–3× a week
  • Walk after you eat
  • A daily wind-down (breathe, walk, see people)
  • Consistent beats extreme — every time
If you remember one thing

You're not managing seven hormones.
You're managing five things.

  1. 1Sleep — biggest lever by miles. Fix this first.
  2. 2Eat enough — fuel, protein, fibre. Under-eating wrecks you just like over-eating.
  3. 3Move + lift — daily steps and weights. Muscle keeps insulin steady and bones strong.
  4. 4Handle your stress — cortisol drags everything down with it. Recovery counts as training.
  5. 5Cut the junk — booze, smoking, crash diets. And know when it's a doctor's job.
